request.question: What is the middle name of the Good Morning America meteoroligist for whom Kait Parker occasionally fills in on weekends?

title: Kevin Ogle
Atlanta Braves Radio Network
Meg Oliver
Steve Grand
Tanya Rivero
Lara Spencer
Kait Parker
Rob Marciano
Chris Vlasto
Laurie Hibberd
sentences: Kevin Ogle is a news anchor for KFOR-TV (channel 4), the NBC affiliate in Oklahoma City, Oklahoma.

Kevin anchors the station's weeknight 6 and 10 p.m. newscasts with Linda Cavanaugh, does occasional reports including the "Bottom Line" financial segments seen during the station's 6:30 p.m. newscast, and occasionally fills in as anchor of the 9 p.m. newscast on Independent station KAUT-TV.
Ogle also serves as moderator of the locally-produced Sunday morning political affairs talk show "Flashpoint", alongside panelists Mike Turpen and former Oklahoma City mayor Kirk Humphreys.

The Atlanta Braves Radio Network is a 138-station network (97 A.M., 41 F.M. stations + 1 F.M. translator) heard across 10 states & one territory of the Southeastern United States that airs Major League Baseball games of the Atlanta Braves.
The flagship stations are WCNN and WYAY in Atlanta, Georgia.
The main announcers are Jim Powell and Don Sutton, who alternate between play-by-play and color commentary on each broadcast.
Ben Ingram is the pregame and postgame host and occasionally fills in on play-by-play, while Kevin McAlpin serves as a dugout reporter.
Mark Lemke provides pregame/postgame analysis and occasionally fills in for Sutton on game broadcasts.
Former known long-time announcers include Pete Van Wieren, Ernie Johnson, Sr. and Skip Caray, all deceased.
The engineer and game producer for Braves Network broadcasts is Brian "Grandma" Giffin.
Network Producers and Operators include Jonathan Chadwick, Chris Culwell, Sean Nerny, Kevin D'Amico, Brian Hoyt, Isiah Stewart and Eric Quintana.
Meg Oliver (born December 7, 1970, Birmingham, Michigan) is an award winning Network Television Correspondent and Anchor.
She returned to CBS News in 2015.

She currently reports for CBS This Morning, the CBS Evening News weekend and fills in anchoring for their 24-hour digital network by CBSN On CBSN she has covered extensive live breaking news from the San Bernardino shootings to the murders of WDBJ's Allison Parker and Adam Ward.
In March 2006, she became anchor of the overnight CBS newscast, "Up to the Minute" and remained in that position for three years.
She was also a correspondent for The Early Show, and fill in anchor.
She left CBS in 2009 and worked at ABC News as a correspondent.
She reported for Good Morning America Weekend Edition and ABC World News with David Muir.
She also filled in anchoring on World News Now.
Steve Grand (born February 28, 1990) is an American singer, songwriter and model from Lemont, Illinois, a Chicago suburb.
He became an overnight internet celebrity and the music video of his first hit "All-American Boy" went viral on YouTube in less than a week in July 2013.
This attention landed Grand on "Good Morning America", CNN and other national media.

On "Good Morning America" Steve shared how difficult it was for him to come out to his parents as a high school student—saying with obvious emotion "I felt like I was a shame to my parents and that there was no way I could ever make them proud."
Buzzfeed ranked the video on its list of the "24 Most Brilliant Music Videos from 2013", and "Out" magazine named Grand to its annual "Out100" list of the year's most compelling LGBT people.
In addition to being a musician, Grand has become an active figure in the LGBT equality movement.
He released his debut album titled "All American Boy" financed by a successful "Kickstarter" public funding campaign.
Tanya Rivero is the host of "Lunch Break" on Wall Street Journal Live since April 2014.
Until August 2013, she was anchor for ABC News Now.
Other work for ABC included hosting Good Morning America Health.
She has filed reports for Good Morning America, Nightline, World News with Diane Sawyer, Weekend World News with David Muir and anchored World News Now and America This Morning.
Before joining ABC News in October 2007, she was a reporter and fill-in anchor for WCBS-TV (CBS 2) in New York City, the flagship station of CBS Television Network.
Lara Christine Von Seelen (known professionally as Lara Spencer) (born June 19, 1969) is an American television presenter.
She is best known for being the co-anchor for ABC's "Good Morning America".
She is also a correspondent for "Nightline" and ABC News.
Previously, she was the host of the syndicated entertainment newsmagazine "The Insider" from 2004 to 2011, and was a regular contributor to CBS's "The Early Show".
Before then, she was the national correspondent for "Good Morning America" and spent several years as a lifestyle reporter for WABC-TV.
She also hosted "Antiques Roadshow" on PBS for the 2004 and 2005 seasons, and "Antiques Roadshow FYI", a spin-off of "Antiques Roadshow", during 2005.
She hosts the show "Flea Market Flip" on both HGTV and the Great American Country channel.
Kait Parker is an atmospheric scientist who currently works for weather.com and The Weather Channel app.
She can also occasionally be seen on "Good Morning America" where she fills-in for meteorologist Rob Marciano on weekends.
Prior to her role with weather.com, she was an on-camera meteorologist and host of America's Morning Headquarters Weekend Edition alongside Reynolds Wolf.
She left The Weather Channel television network in early 2016.
Robert Mark "Rob" Marciano (born June 25, 1968) is an American journalist and meteorologist who is currently employed by ABC News.

Marciano is ABC's, and currently provides forecasts for the weekend editions of "Good Morning America", a position Ginger Zee vacated when she was chosen to succeed Champion on the daily editions of "GMA".
Chris J. Vlasto (born October 27, 1966) is executive producer of Good Morning America.
Prior to that, he was a senior producer at ABC News "20/20" and senior producer of the Law and Justice unit.
Before that he was a senior Broadcast Producer of Good Morning America where he won three Emmy Awards for "Outstanding Morning Program".
He is the son of James Vlasto, former Press Secretary to the New York Governor Hugh Carey and his half brother, Josh Vlasto is currently the Deputy Communications Director to Governor Andrew Cuomo.
Hibberd began her broadcasting career as a traffic reporter, news writer, entertainment reporter and host on CKFM in Toronto from 1987 through 1992.
In the late 80s and early 90s, she also served as a host on YTV for "YTV Rocks" and "Rock'n'Talk".
She later became an entertainment reporter for WSVN-TV in Miami from 1992-1994.
She appeared on "Good Morning America" and "Good Morning America Sunday".
Before that, she co-hosted the FX cable network's morning show, "Breakfast Time", with Tom Bergeron, from 1994-1996.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question` and `passages`, produce the single field `summary`.

Goal: compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a short intermediate state that makes it easy for the next-hop agent to (a) see what facts are present per candidate/entity, (b) see what is ambiguous or missing, and (c) know 1–4 prioritized next-hop retrieval targets or concise queries. This module does not produce the final answer unless the provided passages already unambiguously contain every fact required to resolve the question's relation for every argument.

Procedure:
1) Decide whether the question's relation is semantically complete given the supplied passages. Treat the hop as INCOMPLETE if any of the following hold: a) the relation requires combining or comparing facts across pages (e.g., comparing birth dates, publication/founding years, ordinal ranks, confirming a named director among candidates) and the passages do not supply each required fact unambiguously for all relevant arguments; b) any relation argument is ambiguous or has multiple candidate pages or mentions in the passages; c) an expected candidate page or explicit fact for an argument is missing (e.g., only one side of a comparison is present). Do not trigger INCOMPLETE solely from surface tokens (years, digits) — base the decision on whether the relation's arguments are unambiguously resolved.

2) If the hop is COMPLETE (all required facts to resolve the relation for every argument are present unambiguously in the passages): produce a concise derived result in `summary`. This may be a short phrase or sentence that states the necessary fact(s) needed by the pipeline. Keep it minimal and grounded in the passages; you may include a 1–2 token citation (passage title) if helpful.

3) If the hop is INCOMPLETE or AMBIGUOUS: do NOT assert a final answer or perform the final comparison/selection. Instead emit a short structured report in `summary` containing these labeled elements (prefer bullets or a compact paragraph with explicit labels). Keep all text neutral and non-assertive.

  - evidence: For each named candidate/argument mentioned in the question or passages, list the candidate label and the exact passage title(s) that mention it plus a very short quoted snippet or explicit fact token found there (1–12 words). If a candidate is not mentioned, say so briefly.

  - ambiguities_or_missing: Explicitly name any ambiguities (e.g., multiple candidates for the same name, multiple pages that could be the intended entity) and any missing expected pages or facts.

  - missing_facts_or_bridge: State exactly which fact(s) or disambiguation is needed to complete the relation (e.g., "birth date for Person A", "publication year of Work B", "director credit for Film C"). Be as specific and minimal as possible.

  - retrieval_direction: Provide 1–4 prioritized next-hop targets or concise queries to resolve the missing facts/ambiguities. Prefer explicit page titles when available (exact if known), otherwise provide short search-style queries (few words) or a clear description of the document to retrieve. Order targets by priority.

Formatting and style constraints (apply to both COMPLETE and INCOMPLETE cases):
- The output must be a single `summary` field only. Do not emit other fields.
- In INCOMPLETE reports, use neutral, non-assertive phrasing. Do not produce final comparisons, numeric answers, or definitive selections (e.g., avoid "X is the oldest" or "Answer: 3").
- Keep the report compact and actionable (short labeled bullets or one concise paragraph). Avoid long prose.
- It is allowed and encouraged to quote short factual snippets from passages and cite passage titles to show grounding.
- Do not defer or mark INCOMPLETE when the passages already unambiguously provide all facts for the relation and its arguments.

Produce the `summary` content that follows these rules.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the single field `query`.

Goal
- Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-intent query suitable for a second-hop search that will recover the missing bridging evidence needed to answer the question. Do not produce the final answer in `query`.

Semantic decision rule (when to emit a retrieval-intent query)
- If summary_1 does NOT assert a confirmed linking entity or verified bridging fact needed to answer the question, you MUST emit exactly one compact retrieval-intent query. "Does not assert a confirmed bridge" includes short or candidate-only summaries (e.g., a single named entity, a quoted candidate title, or numeric anchors that are not verified) and any case where the visible anchors do not unambiguously identify the needed bridge.
- If summary_1 already names a confirmed bridge (a clearly identified, verified linking entity or unambiguous metadata that directly connects the question's pieces), you may produce a compact query to fetch supporting evidence, but do NOT produce an answer in the `query` field.
- If summary_1 explicitly states that the bridge cannot be recovered automatically (extreme ambiguity or user-local context requiring clarification), do NOT force a misleading retrieval query. In that case, set `query` to the single short token BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE (all caps, no punctuation) so downstream steps can prompt the user for clarification. Do not invent facts.

Query construction rules
- Output exactly one compact line of text as `query`. Keep it short and retrieval-focused (few keywords, no full sentences). Use visible anchors from `question` and `summary_1` and minimal retrieval keywords that express the missing relation (e.g., anchor1 anchor2 which artist recorded both; AlbumTitle album artist genre; 1991–92 NHL season regular season).
- Do not include the final answer, assertions, or user-facing instructions in the `query` field. Avoid polite phrasing, questions to the user, or explanatory text. Prefer noun phrases and relation keywords.
- If your natural impulse would be to write a declarative answer or a clarification sentence in `query`, instead convert that output into a compact retrieval-intent query that targets the missing bridge.
- Preserve semantic fidelity: base the query only on visible anchors and relations in `question` and `summary_1`. Do not invent hidden identifiers or assume unseen facts.

Format contract
- Return exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short string. If unrecoverable as described above, set `query` to BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE.

Examples of disallowed outputs for `query`
- A declarative answer (e.g., "The artist was X.")
- A user-facing clarification sentence (e.g., "Do you mean X or Y?") unless summary_1 explicitly requires clarification — in that case use BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE.

Behavioral constraints
- Do not use surface heuristics (token count, punctuation, or quotation marks) as the trigger; apply the semantic rule above.
- Emit only one compact retrieval string when the bridge is missing; do not emit multiple queries or long explanations.

Inputs available: `question`, `summary_1`. Output required: single field `query`.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`. The `summary` must be a concise, post-retrieval integration of the evidence that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Follow these rules when composing `summary`:

- Purpose: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` into a grounded state that retains or resolves the earlier bridge only when the second-hop passages supply relation- or attribute-facts that are necessary to answer or disambiguate the `question` (for example: partnership/membership links, joint-award partners indicated by 'with', or requested biographical qualifiers such as nationality, birthdate, current team/status).

- When to append supporting sentence(s): Only append short passage-grounded supporting sentence(s) when the question's semantic intent requires relation or attribute facts to answer or disambiguate. In those cases, produce a compact integrated summary that (a) preserves the concise primary answer or canonical label/token chosen from `context`, and (b) appends one short supporting sentence per relevant entity (or one joint sentence for explicit partnerships) that is directly supported by the supplied `passages` and identity-aligned to the entity referenced in `context`.

- When not to append: Do not append supporting sentences and instead return only the canonical label/token when any of the following apply: the question expects a normalized identifier or single token (examples: binary/multiple-choice label selection between named options, identification-only questions, or explicit token-extraction such as 'middle name'); or the `context` already contains a full, passage-consistent, multi-clause integrated answer. These are semantic exceptions — do not append merely because an answer in `context` is short.

- Identity alignment and verification: Before appending, confirm that the fact in `passages` refers to the same entity referenced in `context` (match names, common aliases, roles, dates, or other corroborating attributes). If identity is ambiguous or unsupported, refrain from appending and preserve uncertainty instead.

- Style and content constraints: Keep output concise. If appending, limit `summary` to 1–3 sentences total (the primary concise answer plus at most one short supporting sentence per relevant entity or one joint supporting sentence). Avoid inventing facts, avoid long verbatim quotations from `passages`, and avoid name-collision errors. Do not change which label or name is selected by the existing answer-selection logic.

- If there is insufficient information in `context` and `passages` to resolve the needed relation/attribute, summarize the uncertainty succinctly rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Produce only the `summary` field as the integrated output string.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use only summary_1 and summary_2 as evidence. Treat summary_2 as the later, integrated evidence and prioritize it when reconciling information; use summary_1 only as the earlier, pre-second-hop context. Do not assume the final state is unresolved solely because summary_1 contains bridging or retrieval-oriented language.

Produce exactly one field, answer, whose value is the minimal final answer string that is fully supported by the visible summaries. The answer field must contain only that minimal plain-text string and nothing else (no explanation, no surrounding quotes, no JSON wrappers, no debugging or transport artifacts).

Decide whether the question's intent is binary (a truth-value / yes-no question) or non-binary (asking for a name, title, date, place, numeric count, selection, or other factual value). Make this decision semantically (based on the question's meaning), not by surface heuristics like token counts or punctuation.

- If the question's intent is binary AND both summary_1 and summary_2 unambiguously agree on the same truth-value, emit exactly one lowercase token: either yes or no (and nothing else) as the answer value.

- For all other question intents (non-binary), emit the minimal factual string supported by the summaries exactly as needed: preserve multi-token values, necessary punctuation, full date formats, and place formats (for example, full names, "City, State", or "Month Day, Year"), and do not truncate these to a single token.

If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not jointly support an unambiguous minimal answer, do not fabricate or over-assert; follow the system's established fallback behavior for ambiguous or unsupported questions.

Always forbid emitting serialized queries, JSON/dict-like structures, internal-debug wrappers, or other transport artifacts as the final answer. The answer must be plain text only.

Make all judgments about minimality and agreement based on the semantic content of summary_1 and summary_2, with priority to summary_2 when they differ.

Produce the answer value exactly as specified above in the answer field.

response.hop1_query: What is the middle name of the Good Morning America meteoroligist for whom Kait Parker occasionally fills in on weekends?
hop1_docs: Kait Parker | Kait Parker is an atmospheric scientist who currently works for weather.com and The Weather Channel app.  She can also occasionally be seen on "Good Morning America" where she fills-in for meteorologist Rob Marciano on weekends. .  Prior to her role with weather.com, she was an on-camera meteorologist and host of America's Morning Headquarters Weekend Edition alongside Reynolds Wolf.  She left The Weather Channel television network in early 2016.

Meg Oliver | Meg Oliver (born December 7, 1970, Birmingham, Michigan) is an award winning Network Television Correspondent and Anchor.  She returned to CBS News in 2015.  She currently reports for CBS This Morning, the CBS Evening News weekend and fills in anchoring for their 24-hour digital network by CBSN On CBSN she has covered extensive live breaking news from the San Bernardino shootings to the murders of WDBJ's Allison Parker and Adam Ward.  In March 2006, she became anchor of the overnight CBS newscast, "Up to the Minute" and remained in that position for three years.  She was also a correspondent for The Early Show, and fill in anchor.  She left CBS in 2009 and worked at ABC News as a correspondent.  She reported for Good Morning America Weekend Edition and ABC World News with David Muir.  She also filled in anchoring on World News Now.

Diane Macedo | Diane Reis Macedo (born February 28, 1982) is an American news personality.  She is currently employed by ABC News where she serves as co-anchor of World News Now and America This Morning.  She also frequently fills-in as the pop news anchor on the weekend version of Good Morning America.  Before coming to ABC News, She was a weekend morning anchor for WCBS-TV in New York City.  Macedo announced her departure from WCBS on March 6, 2016.

Tanya Rivero | Tanya Rivero is the host of "Lunch Break" on Wall Street Journal Live since April 2014.  Until August 2013, she was anchor for ABC News Now.  Other work for ABC included hosting Good Morning America Health.  She has filed reports for Good Morning America, Nightline, World News with Diane Sawyer, Weekend World News with David Muir and anchored World News Now and America This Morning.  Before joining ABC News in October 2007, she was a reporter and fill-in anchor for WCBS-TV (CBS 2) in New York City, the flagship station of CBS Television Network.

Marysol Castro | Marysol Castro is an American broadcast journalist who was employed as a news anchor at WPIX in New York.  She also was a weather forecaster for "The Early Show" on CBS in 2011 and a features correspondent for the weekend edition of "Good Morning America" on ABC from 2004 to 2010.  In June 2015 Castro joined New Haven-based WTNH-TV to fill in for weekday morning traffic reports and anchor until mid-November 2015.

Rob Nelson (reporter) | Rob Nelson (born June 5, 1978) is a weekend morning anchor and weekday reporter at WABC-TV.  Nelson co-anchored the overnight news programs "World News Now" and "America This Morning" on ABC with various co-anchors, including Vinita Nair, Peggy Bunker, Tanya Rivero, Yunji De Nies, Paula Faris, Diana Perez and frequent fill-in Sunny Hostin.  Nelson left on March 15, 2013.  He originally joined ABC on July 12, 2010.  John Muller succeeded Nelson as co-anchor of "World News Now"; Nelson's enormous popularity led to him becoming a regular fill-in for Muller.  In January 2014, Nelson joined WABC-TV as co-anchor of the weekend morning newscasts, replacing Phil Lipof.

Randall Pinkston | Randall Pinkston was a correspondent/anchor for Al Jazeera America.  Previously he was with CBS News.  After a stint as a White House Correspondent in CBS's Washington Bureau, Pinkston became a general assignment reporter, contributing to CBS broadcasts, including CBS Evening News, Morning News, Weekend News, CBS News Sunday Morning and 48 Hours.  Pinkston also contributed to the CBS Reports documentary, Legacy of Shame with Correspondent Dan Rather.  Pinkston has filled in as anchor on the CBS Evening News-Weekend Edition, Up to the Minute and CBS Morning News.
response.summary_1:
- evidence:
  - Kait Parker — passage "Kait Parker": "fills-in for meteorologist Rob Marciano on weekends." (passage [1])
  - Rob Marciano — mentioned only by name in passage [1]; no full name or middle name given.
  - Other passages — none provide Rob Marciano's middle name or a Rob Marciano biography.

- ambiguities_or_missing:
  - Missing Rob Marciano's full legal name / middle name; "Rob" may be a short form (e.g., Robert) but the passages do not confirm this.
  - No dedicated Rob Marciano page or biographical facts are present in the supplied passages.

- missing_facts_or_bridge:
  - The meteorologist's middle name (Rob Marciano's middle name or full name that includes middle name).

- retrieval_direction (priority order):
  1. Wikipedia page "Rob Marciano" (or "Rob Marciano (meteorologist)") — primary target for full name.
  2. ABC / Good Morning America / ABC News biography page for Rob Marciano.
  3. Reliable bio/profile (e.g., The Weather Channel, CNN, LinkedIn, major news profile) with full name.
  4. Search query: "Rob Marciano middle name" or "Rob Marciano full name birth name".
hop2_docs: Rob Marciano | Robert Mark "Rob" Marciano (born June 25, 1968) is an American journalist and meteorologist who is currently employed by ABC News.  Marciano is ABC's, and currently provides forecasts for the weekend editions of "Good Morning America", a position Ginger Zee vacated when she was chosen to succeed Champion on the daily editions of "GMA".

Marciano Art Foundation | The Marciano Art Foundation (formally named the Maurice and Paul Marciano Art Foundation) is a non-profit arts foundation located on Wilshire Boulevard in the Mid-Wilshire neighborhood of Los Angeles, California.  It was established by the co-founders of Guess, Maurice Marciano and Paul Marciano.  The Marcianos plan to open it on May 25, 2017 as an exhibition space to display their 1,500-piece collection of contemporary art.

Marciano Stores | Marciano Stores is an American upscale fashion brand, which is owned and operated by Guess Inc named after Guess's CEO Paul Marciano.  It is the first extension line by Guess and includes clothing such as tops, skirts and dresses, jeans and accessories such as necklaces, earrings and bracelets sold exclusively in Marciano and selected Guess stores.
Roc Marciano | Rakeem Calief Myer (born February 11, 1978), better known by his stage name Roc Marciano, is an American rapper, songwriter, and producer.
Marciano | Marciano is both a surname and a given name.  It originates from Latin "Marcianus" or "Marcian" (Saint Marcians) or "Martians" or "Martianus".  Also from the cult of Roman god Mars.
Léo Marciano | Léo Marciano Paris is a French luxury fashion house founded in 1970 by the eponymous designer Léo Marciano.
Marciano Bruma | Marciano Bruma (born as Marciano van Homoet on 7 March 1984) is a Dutch footballer who currently plays for XerxesDZB in the Dutch Hoofdklasse.
response.summary_2: Mark. Rob Marciano's full name is Robert Mark "Rob" Marciano, confirming his middle name is Mark.
